title: Pre-Application Readiness: What to Do Before You Apply
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A senior QP's guide to licence readiness before an MIA or WDA application: scope, an operating quality system, named persons and a mock inspection.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette deepening into deep navy shadows, lit by soft natural daylight with a single subtle green accent glow. The scene is a quality-assurance review desk inside a pristine, glass-walled facility: a gloved hand rests beside a neatly bound site master file, a sleek tablet displaying abstract qualification and audit-readiness charts, and a fountain pen poised over a signature page, while out-of-focus stainless-steel cleanroom equipment recedes behind glass. Immaculate reflective surfaces, calm and authoritative atmosphere, a sense of final checks before a decision, shallow depth of field with creamy bokeh, premium and sterile yet warm, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: How do I know if my site is ready to apply for an MIA or WDA?
faqs.a: You are ready when every claim in your application is backed by objective evidence that the system is already operating, not merely written. In practice that means version-controlled SOPs with real usage records, a populated deviation and CAPA log, at least one completed self-inspection cycle, qualified premises and equipment, and named persons in post with documented authority and deputy cover. If you cannot produce a record for each procedure you have written, you are not yet ready.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a documented and a demonstrated quality system?
faqs.a: A documented system exists on paper: procedures, a quality manual and templates that describe how work should be done. A demonstrated system has generated records that prove the work is actually being done that way, such as completed deviations, executed change controls and training evidence. MHRA inspectors assess the demonstrated system, so the records matter far more than the prose.

faqs.q: Should I run a mock inspection before applying for a licence?
faqs.a: Yes. An independent mock inspection or gap assessment simulates the MHRA pre-licensing inspection and surfaces weaknesses your own team has grown blind to, testing both your documents and your people. Every gap you close beforehand is a finding the regulator will not raise, and remediating in calm conditions is far cheaper than doing so against an inspection clock. Applicants who invest in a realistic dry run typically reach licence grant faster and with fewer post-inspection actions.
